Attempting to load Google N-gram data via the perl driver and unable to store large integers.

$db->insert( { count => 2712631400 } ) stores  -1582336458

I'd be more than happy to test alpha code.

Thanks, Jeff

Hi Kristina,

The compilation ends in failure using the compilers not supporting C99, such as MSVC7.0.
This is because of the violation of a rule that declarations must precede statements.
We must conform to the rule in C89, which, in my opinion, is the real C.
So, may I ask you a favour?  Could you apply the attached patch?

Best regards,
Taro Nishino

summary: Cursor cleanup issues when connection is broken
accept an arbitrary number of servers on connection
description: http://www.mongodb.org/display/DOCS/Connections

on a collection that's about 20GB with ~500,000 records

for some queries, $cursor->next() will reproducibly fail, always at the same point for a given query.

Sometimes $collection->count() will appear to timeout and then complain:

Use of uninitialized value in pattern match (m//) at /usr/lib64/perl5/site_perl/5.8.8/x86_64-linux-thread-multi/MongoDB/Collection.pm line 348.

When repeated a second time, the call usually succeeds.

t/database.t .... 6/14 
#   Failed test 'last error timeout'
#   at t/database.t line 52.
#          got: 'HASH(0xdbae020)'
#     expected: 'timed out waiting for slaves'
# Looks like you failed 1 test of 14.
t/database.t .... Dubious, test returned 1 (wstat 256, 0x100)
maybe clean up tutorial a bit, too

I've noticed that the save() method ignores the 'safe' option. Looks like it's just a small mixup in a conditional. Can you please check the attached patch? I don't know if all server versions throw the same exception when you try to save to the 'system. indexes' collection so there could be a chance that the test doesn't work with all server versions.

The JSON and JSON::XS modules require a blessed object to provide a TO_JSON method if you want your object serialized.  I believe it would be helpful if MongoDB::OID provided a TO_JSON method that returns either a simple quoted string or else a string representation of a JSON object, whichever would be more consonant with the JSON reported by the Mongo shell and/or the internal BSON.

summary: The driver is not thread safe
description:
The following Perl script cause a crash of the MongoDB driver:

#!/usr/bin/perl

use strict;
use warnings;
use MongoDB;
use threads;

my $conn = MongoDB::Connection->new(host => 'localhost', port => 27017);
my $db = $conn->get_database('test');
my $coll = $db->get_collection('test');

my $tid = threads->create(\&test_thread);
$tid->detach();

sleep 1;

$coll->insert({ 'data' => 1}); # just some dummy data

exit;

sub test_thread {
}

If the cursor timeout hits, e.g. due to network outage, the connection is not closed nor is the state updated to cope with delayed response should it arrive later.  This could cause Trouble, even corruption, as the responses may up out of sync by one operation.
"local $MongoDB::Cursor::timeout = <n>" is a bit kludgey, wouldn't you say?  :-)

summary: Allow creating Database and Collection objects when disconnected
description: The current driver is quite good at coping with the server shutting down and restarting, but it doesn't apparently cope so well with the server not being up when the client starts.  I would like the Database and Collection objects to be createable in a speculative way, i.e. with the assumption that when the server comes up they'll be there.
Using bulk_insert, it is possible to create documents that contain attributes containing "."
Protect against "$foo" errors

summary: boolean::true and boolean::false are accepted to put boolean values in, but they are not returned to get boolean values back out
description:
(I posted this on the CPAN bugtracker before I realized the Perl Driver had its own section in JIRA, so my apologies.  The original can be found here: https://rt.cpan.org/Public/Bug/Display.html?id=60161 )

To place boolean values "true" and "false" into MongoDB using insert,  save, etc., boolean::true and boolean::false can be used.

However, when these boolean values are read back out, they are translated into 1 and 0, which are functionally equivalent, but somewhat defeat the purpose of using boolean::true and boolean::false to save the values in the first place.

To solve this, I have provided the following patch, which will attempt to call boolean::true or boolean::false appropriately for BSON_BOOL items returned from the database (and if those fail, hopefully fall back on the current behavior).

This is my first time writing C to call a Perl function, so comments or suggestions are very much welcome.  All I can say is that this is working great for me.

Hi Kristina,

The compilation ends in failure using the compilers not supporting C99, such as MSVC7.0, just because the author of MongoDB-0.36 is in complete ignorance of the C89 rules.
Don't get me wrong. Besides, the author doesn't seem to know the following:
1. The meaning of the dSP declaration.
2. Inline functions should be defined in header files. The thing is, this is a common sense.

So, may I ask you a favour?  Could you apply the attached patches?

Best regards,
Taro Nishino

summary: 0.36 build failure when packaging for Debian / Ubuntu systems
description: Building the driver for a .deb package fails when running the tests. The part of the build output where it fails on a Lucid system looks like this:

no ok 28 - slurp
#   Failed test 'slurp'
#   in t/gridfs.t at line 110

Also, is 0.35 considered unreleased?

summary: cursor not found croak() bubbling up after upgrade?
description:
I had been running a MySQL migration tool that I wrote using the 0.33 driver against various releases of MongoDB 1.6.x using auto-sharding and 2 shards backed my 3 machine replica sets.  Today I upgraded to 1.6.2 and decided to upgrade to the latest (0.36 Perl driver).  I did a full wipe of all my data, installed 1.6.2, and tried to run my script.

It now fails on the first record with "cursor not found" being caught by my die handler.

I see this commit:

http://github.com/mongodb/mongo-perl-driver/commit/4305664d2ae1b5c67cae0beff256d1173b6ac95b

But I'm not sure if this is a long-standing bug in my code that this upgrade revealed (despite it having inserted millions of records in testing) or if something else weird is going on.  The replica sets are healthy, as are the mongos processes and config servers.  In fact, the first record *does* get inserted before it blows up.

summary: not all modules end with a "true value"

description:
Hi Kristina,

As might have been expected, the compilation still ends in failure because the dSP declaration is presence after the "if" statement of the block of "case BSON_BOOL:" in perl_mongo.c, which beats the shit out of C89 rules.
Take a look at perl_mongo.c.diff.txt that I sent before, please.

Best regards,
Taro Nishino

summary: gridfs throws exception if prefix is not "fs"
description:
The Perl code for gridfs insertion runs the "filemd5" command against a root of "fs", even if a different prefix is chosen.
If there isn't an "fs.chunks" collection in the database, this causes an exception:

Assertion: 13284:best guess plan requested, but scan and order required: query: { files_id: ObjectId('...') } order: { files_id: 1, n: 1 } choices: { $natural: 1 } 

This is complaining about the lack of an index which is correct because it's been given the wrong collection prefix.

Didn't notice the problem until I upgraded the server from 1.4.x to 1.6.x, but it looks from the change history like it's been hardcoded to "fs" since the beginning.

I've developed and attached a patch against 0.37.  In order to do it correctly, I've added the prefix as an attribute of the GridFS object and pass it down on creation.  I've also taken the opportunity to refactor the code so that the chunks and files collections are built by the GridFS object itself rather than inside the Database object as that seems more sensible to me - it keeps the code in one place.  I've also added a filename index to the files collection as this mimics the behaviour of the "mongofiles" client and was missing from the Perl code originally.

I hope my changes are acceptable;  I admit they go a little bit beyond just fixing the bug, but not my much and hopefully they make sense.

Cheers,
Olly

PS> apologies for attaching the diff; don't have a github account right now; if you'd rather receive a merge request, I'll set one up.

summary: Cannot create an index on a sub-document
description:
Problem:

As reported by user

I've got a problem with the last version of the Perl driver. 
I used to create indexes with embedded keys, like this: 
$coll->ensure_index( { "address.state" => 1 } ); 
Since the following commit I cannot use the dot notation anymore. It 
fails with "inserts cannot contain the . character". 
http://github.com/mongodb/mongo-perl-driver/commit/0baf83a0e0b5df255d... 
Shouldn't the "." char be allowed for the inserts in the index collection ? 

Per the documentation, the dot notation should be valid

http://www.mongodb.org/display/DOCS/Indexes#Indexes-EmbeddedKeys


Resolution:
Enable users to create indexes on embedded documents

Business Case:
Backward compatibility
The documentation doesn't actually mention that with  safe=>1  various methods will throw exceptions.  This makes for an unpleasant surprise.

summary: The ->update method should return count of modified objects, at least if safe=>1
description: In safe mode, ->update has enough information to return the count of affected documents.  It's easy to make the function return true for success and also a zero count; just return '0E0' like DBI does.

summary: Can't find documentation on return value of last_error
description: What's supposed to be returned by last_error?  The documentation doesn't say.

summary: croak on serialization if obj type is unrecognized
Can't locate object method "new" via package "MongoDB::Timestamp"
description:
I'm reproducibly getting the error:

Can't locate object method "new" via package "MongoDB::Timestamp" (perhaps you forgot to load "MongoDB::Timestamp"?) at /usr/lib64/perl5/site_perl/5.8.8/x86_64-linux-thread-multi/MongoDB/Collection.pm line 514.

The offending line in my code is:

   $message_collection->insert( {recipient=>$recipient, message=>$message, param=>$opt_param}, {safe=>1} );

The data does wind up in the collection, though:

> db.messages.find()
{ "_id" : ObjectId("4cf7ce1b67458bcf7d000000"), "recipient" : "joshr", "param" : "", "message" : "HI" }
>

I noticed from an older bug report (perl-102) that a similar bug once something to do with masters/slaves.

The server I'm connecting to is set up as a master but has no slaves connected currently.

summary: Floating timezone handling may be surprising when converting from MySQL
description:
When inserting DateTime objects, the time is correctly adjusted to UTC and converted to epoch. However, if the floating timezone specified, the time is assumed to be UTC and no adjustments are made. 

This is almost certainly not what you want, particularly when writing scripts to migrate from MySQL -- DateTime::Format::MySQL returns floating timezone objects. 

When deflating, I would either assume that floating timezone means local, or emit a warning, since there's really no situation in which you'd use DateTime::TimeZone::Floating to mean UTC.

The Perl driver should determine the maximum BSON document size by calling isMaster at connection-time.

summary: Segmentation Fault when inserting/updating values with exotic utf8 string as keys
description:
Pretty much everything is in the summary. The bug should be easily reproduced using the attached perl script.

Here is the gdb output:
0x00007fee84bb12e0 in hv_to_bson (buf=0x7fff10cc9660, sv=<value optimized out>, ids=0x25f3ed0, stack=<value optimized out>, is_insert=1) at perl_mongo.c:924

Execute:
my $id = $collection->insert({"a.a"=>"1"});

Get an error:
inserts cannot contain the . character at /****/perl/lib/site_perl/5.14.0/x86_64-linux/MongoDB/Collection.pm line 303.

summary: Databases/collections created during MongoDB module install from CPAN
description:
Test databases/collections created (one of which was "affe"/"tiger", and the other was "test", but I can't remember the name of the collection) during the CPAN install for the MongoDB driver module.

I was slightly alarmed, thinking someone was playing a joke on me (this was a production server, out in the wild), until I realized it may have been from the tests from the Perl driver install. Once I realized that was a possibility, it made a lot more sense, and I started to be less concerned about the security of my server.

In any case, I imagine that these tests are useful, but maybe they shouldn't be run from the default CPAN install (so they don't frighten paranoid people like me). The driver has been working great out of the box, by the way.

summary: Change connection and recv code to handle timeout properly in all cases

description:
Hi Kristina, 

The compilation ends in failure using MSVC7.0.
So, may I ask you a favour? Could you apply the attached patches? 

Best regards, 
Taro Nishino
